one_line_takeaway: WMVLM trains a Qwen3-VL-8B model via SFT + distillation-based cold start + GRPO to jointly score and explain both residual and semantic diffusion-model watermarks' quality and robustness, sharply outperforming 11 zero-shot closed- and open-source VLM baselines on correlation/accuracy metrics across in-distribution, cross-dataset, cross-model, and cross-method settings.
key_finding: WMVLM substantially outperforms all 11 baseline VLMs on both quality correlation and security/quality accuracy across every watermark method tested. In the main comparison (Table 1), WMVLM reaches PLCC/SRCC above 0.7 for 5 of 6 residual methods (0.885-0.983 PLCC, except HiDDeN at 0.712) with residual security accuracy 98.8-100%, versus baselines mostly clustering near 0 (e.g., GPT-5 PLCC 0.064-0.211, LLaVA-v1.6-7B often near-zero or negative). For semantic watermarks WMVLM's quality/security accuracy ranges 0.852-0.956 (RingID 0.852/0.852, Tree-Ring 0.956/0.956, Lossless 0.910/0.910) vs. best baselines typically under 0.7 (e.g., Gemini-3-Pro Lossless 0.716/0.716). Generalization holds up: cross-dataset (MS-COCO) shows only a ~10-point accuracy drop for RingID with other methods largely stable; cross-model (SD v1.4) shows Stable Signature PLCC drop of ~0.6 (0.981->0.368) attributed to its generation-integrated watermark being architecture-sensitive, while other methods stay high; cross-method testing on 3 unseen watermarking schemes (GaussMarker, T2SMark, Ori) keeps all WMVLM accuracies above 87%. The ablation (Table 5) shows each of the three stages is necessary: omitting Stage 1 (score pretraining) prevents the model from learning discriminative watermark features at all; Stage 2 alone (cold start without S1) degrades performance versus S1-only on several methods (e.g. lower PLCC on RobustWide/VINE, lower RingID/Lossless accuracy) because SFT-based explanation generation without prior score grounding introduces noise; Stage 3 (GRPO) recovers/improves over the S1+S2 dip, most visibly for RivaGAN and HiDDeN PLCC/SRCC, supporting the claim that GRPO mitigates SFT-induced pattern rigidity.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is WMVLM, a vision-language-model (VLM) framework for evaluating diffusion-model image watermarks, unifying two previously disparate evaluation paradigms (residual watermarks vs. semantic/latent watermarks) into a single interpretable scoring model. It (1) redefines quality/security scoring per watermark type (PSNR-based quality plus binary robustness-to-OSN-distortion labels for residual watermarks; latent-distribution hypothesis-testing p-values, discretized into 3-level quality/security scores, for semantic watermarks), and (2) trains a VLM (Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct backbone) via a three-stage pipeline: Stage 1 SFT for category/score prediction, Stage 2 knowledge-distillation 'cold start' from Gemini-2.5-Pro to produce free-text interpretable explanations, and Stage 3 GRPO reinforcement learning with a composite reward (format, category, response-length, quality, security terms) to reduce SFT pattern rigidity and improve generalization.
threat_model: The security dimension the paper evaluates (not proposes as a novel attack) is watermark robustness: for residual watermarks, resistance to three classical OSN-style distortions (JPEG compression, Gaussian noise, median filtering); for semantic watermarks, resistance to erasure/detectability risk inferred from how far the watermarked latent distribution deviates from the standard normal N(0,I) prior, citing prior adversarial-example-based erasure attacks (An et al. 2024; Lin & Juarez 2025 'A crack in the bark') as the motivating threat that WMVLM's metric is meant to proxy for. WMVLM itself does not mount new attacks; it is an evaluator/judge model that scores existing watermarking schemes' quality and security post hoc.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Conclusion section): WMVLM still depends on SFT and therefore requires substantial curated training data despite adding GRPO; they suggest future work could pursue RL methods that reduce SFT dependence. They also note the framework is scoped to image watermarking in diffusion models only, and extending to other modalities is left as future work. Observed in the paper's own results but not explicitly flagged as a limitation in prose: semantic-watermark quality/security accuracy (85-96%) is consistently lower than residual-watermark security accuracy (~99%), which the authors attribute to the more subtle/latent nature of semantic watermark signals; and WMVLM's cross-model PLCC for Stable Signature falls sharply (0.981 to 0.368) when the diffusion backbone changes from SD v2.1 to SD v1.4, indicating sensitivity to architecture changes for watermarks integrated deeply into the generation pipeline.

datasets_used: Images generated with Stable Diffusion v2.1 using prompts from Stable-Diffusion-Prompts-2.47M (5,000 images for 6 residual watermarking methods, 9,500 images for semantic/lossless watermarking methods; 500 held out per method for test)

9 watermarking methods: residual = DwtDct, RivaGAN, HiDDeN, RobustWide (RW), VINE, Stable Signature (SS); semantic/lossless = RingID, Tree-Ring, and a 'Lossless' group (Gaussian Shading, PRCW, Gaussian Shading++)
500 Gemini-2.5-Pro-generated post-hoc interpretable explanations per method used for the Stage-2 cold-start distillation
Cross-dataset generalization set: 500 images per method generated with prompts sampled from MS-COCO Validation Set
Cross-model generalization set: 500 images per method generated with SD v1.4 using the original test prompts
Cross-method generalization set: 500 images per method for GaussMarker, T2SMark, and unwatermarked originals (Ori)
quantitative_results: Main comparison (Table 1), WMVLM: residual PLCC/SRCC/security-accuracy -- DwtDct 0.952/0.957/0.988, RivaGAN 0.885/0.875/0.990, HiDDeN 0.712/0.708/0.998, RW 0.961/0.973/0.994, VINE 0.983/0.986/1.000, SS 0.981/0.985/0.998; semantic quality/security accuracy -- RingID 0.852/0.852, Tree-Ring 0.956/0.956, Lossless 0.910/0.910

Best closed-source baseline residual PLCC values are near zero to ~0.27 (e.g., GPT-5 0.064-0.211, Claude-Opus-4.5 -0.139 to 0.272, Gemini-3-Pro 0.043-0.19); best open-source baseline residual PLCC also weak/unstable (e.g., LLaVA-v1.6-7B ranges -0.098 to 0.741 but with near-zero security accuracy)
Cross-dataset (Table 2): WMVLM RingID accuracy drops ~10 points (0.852->0.746) while other methods remain comparable to Table 1 (e.g., DwtDct PLCC 0.952->0.933)
Cross-model (Table 3, SD v1.4): Stable Signature PLCC drops from 0.981 to 0.368 (~0.6 decrease), other residual/semantic methods stay largely comparable (e.g., VINE 0.978/0.982, RingID 0.792/0.792)

Cross-method (Table 4): WMVLM accuracy on GaussMarker 0.956/0.956, T2SMark 0.930/0.930, Ori (unwatermarked) 0.874/0.874 -- all baseline methods substantially lower (best baseline on GaussMarker: Gemma-3-12B 0.684/0.678; on T2SMark: Qwen3-VL-32B 0.906/0.890)

Ablation (Table 5): S1-only gives near-zero/negative PLCC for most residual methods (e.g., DwtDct 0.048, HiDDeN -0.057) and low semantic accuracy (RingID 0.106/0.106); S1+S2 without S3 is worse than S1-only on some methods (e.g., DwtDct PLCC drops to -0.036, RivaGAN semantic-analog features degrade) but full S1+S2+S3 recovers/best performance matches Table 1 main results
baselines_compared: Closed-source zero-shot VLMs: GPT-5, Claude-Opus-4.5, Gemini-3-Pro
Open-source zero-shot VLMs: LLaVA-v1.6 (7B, 13B), Qwen3-VL (4B, 8B, 32B), Gemma-3 (4B, 12B, 27B)
